# LLMs in Evidence Synthesis and Literature Review

## Automated Literature Screening

The exponential growth of biomedical literature has made manual systematic review increasingly impractical, with large reviews requiring hundreds of reviewer-hours for title and abstract screening alone. Large language models have emerged as a promising solution to this bottleneck, offering scalable, consistent screening capacity that can substantially reduce the time and personnel costs associated with evidence synthesis. Early evaluations have positioned LLMs not as replacements for human judgment but as intelligent pre-filters capable of dramatically narrowing candidate pools before expert adjudication.

Studies consistently demonstrate that GPT-4 and comparable frontier models achieve high sensitivity—critical for systematic reviews where missed studies represent systematic bias—while offering only modest specificity [@Lee2025ChatGPT]. LLM screening excels when configured to minimize false negatives, accepting a higher false-positive burden in exchange for near-complete retrieval. This asymmetric optimization aligns with systematic review methodology, where recall is prioritized over precision. In practice, pipelines pairing LLM pre-screening with single-reviewer verification of excluded records have demonstrated time savings of 40–60% compared to conventional dual-reviewer workflows [@Lim2026How]. Specialized research platforms such as Genspark and Scispace, alongside GPT-4o, Claude 3, and Gemini Pro, have each demonstrated distinct performance profiles: GPT-4o and o1 excel in breadth of coverage, Claude 3 in structured text handling, and Gemini Pro in multimodal document processing [@Lee2025ChatGPT], suggesting that optimal pipelines may combine models rather than relying on a single LLM.

## Data Extraction from Published Studies

Beyond screening, extraction of structured quantitative data from published manuscripts represents a high-value but error-prone step in evidence synthesis. Manual data extraction for meta-analyses is notoriously inconsistent, with inter-extractor agreement often below 90% for numerical outcomes, and errors propagate directly into pooled effect estimates. LLMs offer the potential to standardize this process while reducing the labor intensity of large-scale evidence aggregation.

A rigorous comparative evaluation of four LLMs—DeepSeek v3 R1, Claude 3.5 Sonnet, ChatGPT-4o, and Gemini 2.0-flash—for numeric data extraction from full-text dental research PDFs demonstrated that extraction accuracy at the single data-point level was exceptionally high, with only three hallucinations recorded (all from Gemini 2.0-flash) across the entire evaluation corpus [@Caponio2026Accuracy]. Gemini 2.0-flash performed significantly worse than its competitors overall (p < 0.01), while Claude 3.5 Sonnet and DeepSeek-v3 R1 exhibited superior accuracy and lower omission rates in full-text extraction tasks. Critically, however, error rates increased substantially when data aggregation moved from the sub-outcome to the outcome and study levels, indicating that while single-point extraction is reliable, compound data structures—such as composite endpoints, subgroup analyses, or time-series measurements—remain challenging and require human verification.

Extraction of PICO (Population, Intervention, Comparator, Outcome) elements from randomized trial reports has similarly attracted attention as a structured task well suited to LLM capabilities. Neuro-symbolic approaches that couple GPT-4 with rule-based expert systems have demonstrated particular promise for clinical report parsing. In a study of 206 consecutive prostate cancer PET/CT scan reports requiring extraction of 26 parameters per report (generating 5,356 data points), standalone GPT-4 achieved F1 scores of 0.63 for study inclusion and 0.95 for recurrence detection, with 96.6% accuracy for PSA value retrieval [@Prenosil2025Neurosymbolic]. Physicians achieved F1 scores of 1.00 and 0.99 respectively, with 98.1% PSA accuracy—a gap that the neuro-symbolic hybrid closed entirely, achieving F1 scores of 1.00 on both tasks with 100% PSA accuracy while also providing an auditable chain of reasoning for each decision. This architecture additionally intercepted two intentionally introduced reports containing residual patient identifiers, preventing inadvertent privacy breaches—a capability absent from standalone LLM pipelines. These findings underscore the value of hybrid architectures that leverage LLM flexibility for unstructured text while enforcing medical rule compliance through deterministic post-processing.

## Systematic Review Automation

The vision of end-to-end LLM-assisted systematic review has moved from speculative to partially realized. Contemporary pipelines treat each stage of the review as a modular LLM task, with structured handoffs between screening, extraction, and synthesis agents. Scoping reviews of AI applications in specific clinical domains—such as the 114-study analysis of AI in cardiac arrest management encompassing data from 9,574,462 patients [@Luo2026Beyond]—exemplify how LLM-assisted evidence mapping can systematically characterize a rapidly evolving literature that would be prohibitively large for traditional manual methods.

Quality assessment automation represents a particularly important frontier. Assessing risk of bias using tools such as the Cochrane RoB 2.0 or ROBINS-I frameworks requires nuanced judgment about methodological adequacy—precisely the kind of structured reasoning that benefits from LLM assistance. A systematic review of LLM applications in obesity management, which included 33 studies evaluated with the ROBINS-I tool, demonstrated that LLM-assisted quality assessment could be operationalized across heterogeneous designs spanning personalized nutrition, surgical care, pharmacotherapy, and motivational interviewing, though substantial variability across sub-domains was noted [@Suenghataiphorn2026Large].

Compliance with reporting guidelines such as PRISMA, CONSORT, and STROBE is a persistent challenge, and LLMs have been deployed to automate adherence checking. The REFINE checklist—developed by a 57-contributor international consortium across 17 countries—provides a 44-item, six-section reporting standard for FM and LLM studies, directly enabling reliable evidence synthesis for this rapidly proliferating research domain [@Mese2026Reporting]. In a parallel domain, ChatGPT-4 demonstrated 70% concordance with NCCN guidelines and 60% with ESGO guidelines in gynecologic oncology decision support, with an overall recommendation accuracy of 75%; consultation platforms remained less accurate than expert clinicians (60% vs. 86.7%) but outperformed static guideline-based answers [@Rosati2026Natural].

A systematic review of 39 medical LLM benchmarks—encompassing more than 2.3 million questions across 45 languages and 172 medical specialties—found that leading models achieve 84–90% accuracy on USMLE-style knowledge examinations, yet practice-based assessments show substantially lower performance (45–69%), with clinical reasoning dropping to 50–60% and diagnostic task success at 45–55% [@Gong2025KnowledgePractice]. For evidence synthesis, this knowledge-practice gap implies that LLMs are well suited for pattern-recognition tasks such as screening and structured extraction, but require careful human oversight for higher-order synthesis requiring clinical reasoning.

## Patient Education Materials

The generation of health education materials represents a high-volume, high-value application of LLMs that intersects with evidence synthesis: clinically accurate patient-facing content must reflect the current evidence base while being accessible to lay audiences. A systematic review and meta-analysis of ChatGPT and other LLMs in dental patient education, conducted following PRISMA guidelines and encompassing 25 studies from seven databases, found a pooled accuracy score of 81.87% (95% CI: 77.24%–86.51%) across all included LLMs, with 69.9% (95% CI: 57.3%–82.6%) of responses rated as clinically acceptable [@Zhang2025Leveraging]. Subgroup analysis revealed that ChatGPT-3.5 accuracy was significantly higher than Microsoft Bing, though not statistically different from ChatGPT-4.0 or Google Bard, suggesting that model generation matters less than task-appropriate prompting strategies for patient-facing content.

A living systematic review of patient-facing LLM chatbots in older adult care, which screened 1,228 records and included 9 studies with a median sample size of 12 older participants, found that GPT-3.5 was the most frequently deployed LLM for supportive and social-emotional conversation interventions, with a median Technology Readiness Level of 7 [@Johnson2026Research]. Notably, no included chatbot achieved a readiness level of 8 or higher—the threshold for demonstrated effectiveness—and no study evaluated clinical or cost-effectiveness under fully deployed clinical workflow conditions, indicating that the field remains in exploratory phases despite enthusiasm for the technology.

Multilingual content generation represents a further dimension of LLM value for patient education, particularly in health systems serving linguistically diverse populations. The ability of frontier LLMs to produce medically accurate content across dozens of languages without professional translation holds considerable promise for health equity. However, accuracy assessments conducted primarily in English may not transfer to lower-resource languages, and the 45 languages represented across 39 benchmarks reviewed by Gong et al. [@Gong2025KnowledgePractice] illustrate that geographic and linguistic representation remains incomplete. Systematic evaluation of readability, accuracy, and cultural appropriateness of LLM-generated multilingual health materials remains a priority as these tools move toward broader clinical deployment.
